# Prefer Small Reversible Changes

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

Engineering teams SHOULD decompose changes into the smallest units that
can be independently deployed and independently rolled back. When a change
cannot be made reversible, it SHOULD receive proportionally more review,
testing, and staged rollout.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C26 (Change as Primary Risk)** — Every change to a running system
  is a risk event. Smaller changes bound the blast radius; reversibility
  bounds the recovery time. A change that is both small and reversible
  converts a potential crisis into a routine correction.
- **JOS-C21 (System Entropy)** — Systems accumulate disorder over time.
  Large irreversible changes accelerate entropy because their second-order
  effects are harder to predict and harder to unwind. Small reversible
  changes let teams observe entropy as it forms and correct course before
  it compounds.

## Why This Matters

Irreversible changes force organizations into forward-only problem solving:
you cannot retreat, so you must fix forward under pressure. This mode is
expensive, error-prone, and stressful. Reversible changes give teams a
safety net — the option to return to a known-good state while investigating.
The preference for smallness is equally important: a small change is easier
to reason about, easier to review, and easier to attribute when something
goes wrong.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Not every change can be small. Database schema migrations, API contract
changes, and infrastructure replacements sometimes require coordinated,
large-scale moves. The principle does not forbid these — it asks teams to
prefer the reversible path when one exists and to apply extra rigor when
it does not. There is also a cost to extreme decomposition: too many tiny
changes can create integration risk and coordination overhead. The goal
is the sweet spot where each change is comprehensible to a single reviewer
and revertable without cascading effects.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, deploying software meant shipping physical media; rollback was
nearly impossible. In 2070, deployment mechanisms will differ radically,
but the physics of change risk will not: large irreversible mutations
remain more dangerous than small reversible ones regardless of tooling.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.

### Framework Connection

Framework §14 (blast radius reasoning with Direction/Reach/Reversibility dimensions) gives this principle a structured assessment model — every change's risk is scored across three axes, making the preference for small reversible changes operationally measurable.
